title: HVAC Refrigerant Retrofits & Replacements Guide
intended_use: Decision support for homeowners and HVAC technicians about R-22 systems, retrofit options, and regulatory changes.

assistant_behavior: Always emphasize that refrigerant handling requires EPA Section 608 certification
Never coach homeowners through adding/charging refrigerant
If system is leaking, prioritize leak repair or replacement over 'topping off'

r22_phase_out.why_phased_out: R-22 (HCFC-22) depletes the ozone layer. Phased out under Clean Air Act and Montreal Protocol.

timeline.2010: Ban on new equipment manufacturing using R-22
timeline.2020: Total ban on production and import of virgin R-22 in US
timeline.current: Only recycled/reclaimed R-22 available. Supplies finite and shrinking.

r22_phase_out.pricing_reality: Extremely high and volatile (often 5-10x cost of modern refrigerants). Prices vary by region and supplier.
r22_phase_out.implication: Repairing a major R-22 leak is often economically unviable compared to system replacement.

replacement_options.critical_warning: NEVER MIX REFRIGERANTS. System must be recovered and evacuated first. 'Drop-in' means compatible with existing oil, NOT mixable with R-22.

drop_in_replacements.description: Compatible with mineral oil in older R-22 systems. No oil change typically required.

R-438A_MO99.best_for: Most residential AC retrofits where oil change is too costly

R-422D_MO29.best_for: Low/medium temp refrigeration, less ideal for high-load residential AC

R-417A.best_for: Specific legacy systems with known good performance

near_drop_in.description: Requires POE oil. Since R-22 units have mineral oil, an oil change/flush is required (labor intensive).

cons: REQUIRES POE oil - will cause compressor failure if used with mineral oil
R-407C.best_for: Commercial systems or when technician is willing to change oil

cons: More common in refrigeration than comfort cooling

reasons: Operates at pressures ~50% higher than R-22
Would cause catastrophic equipment failure (burst coils/compressor)
Uses POE oil vs R-22's mineral oil
Components not rated for R-410A pressures
R-410A.assistant_response: R-410A cannot be used in R-22 equipment. The pressures are incompatible and would cause equipment failure and safety risks.

steps: Confirm system condition: leak history, compressor health, coil condition
Recover all existing R-22 (do NOT vent - illegal)
Repair leaks FIRST - retrofit into leaking system is wasted money
Evaluate lubricant compatibility; perform oil change if required
Charge with new refrigerant (LIQUID charge for blends due to glide)
Commission using appropriate PT charts for retrofit refrigerant
Label system with new refrigerant type, oil type, and charge amount

performance_expectations.capacity: Expect 5-10% loss in cooling power with most retrofits
performance_expectations.seals: Elastomer O-rings may shrink after removing R-22, causing leaks

decision_framework.quick_rule: If system is old, inefficient, or has confirmed leak, replacement usually delivers better long-term value.
replace_system_if: Repair cost > 50% of new system cost
System has needed refrigerant multiple times (chronic leak)
retrofit_can_work_if: System is mechanically healthy and leak-free (or leak repaired once)
System is relatively newer (rare for R-22 at this point)
Owner needs short-term bridge while planning replacement
Budget is strictly limited and compressor is healthy

cost_comparison.retrofit_cost: Service call + leak repair + drier + recovery + new refrigerant ($500-$1,500+)
cost_comparison.risk: If compressor dies 6 months later, retrofit money is wasted
cost_comparison.owner_guidance: If paying for refrigerant more than once, the system is telling you it's time for replacement

characteristics.compatibility: Cannot retrofit R-22 equipment to use R-410A

aim_act.goal: Phase down high-GWP HFCs (including R-410A) by 85% by 2036

timeline.2025_jan: Ban on manufacturing NEW residential/light commercial systems using R-410A
timeline.2026_jan: Installation deadline for some equipment manufactured before cutoff

R-454B.notes: Requires equipment designed for A2L, leak sensors, updated safety standards

impact_on_current_owners: Existing R-410A equipment can still be serviced
R-410A will become more expensive over time (like R-22 did)
No requirement to replace functioning systems solely due to policy
When replacing, expect A2L refrigerant options

epa_section_608.requirement: Federal law requires EPA certification to buy or handle refrigerants
epa_section_608.fines: Up to $44,539 per day for violations (venting refrigerant)
epa_section_608.homeowner_restriction: Homeowners cannot legally purchase R-22 or R-410A without certification

safety_and_regulations.venting_prohibition: Illegal. Refrigerants must be recovered into specialized tank and sent to reclaimer.
safety_and_regulations.environmental_impact: Improper handling damages ozone layer (R-22) or contributes to climate change (HFCs)

reasons: Dangerous - refrigerants can cause frostbite and displace oxygen
Requires specialized tools (manifolds, scales, recovery equipment)
Topping off without fixing leak is temporary and wasteful
can_i_add_freon_myself.what_to_do: Call licensed HVAC technician to find/repair leak, then charge correctly

my_system_uses_r22_options.option_1: Pay high price for reclaimed R-22 (best performance, temporary solution)
my_system_uses_r22_options.option_2: Retrofit to MO99 or similar (cheaper refrigerant, labor cost, slight performance loss)
my_system_uses_r22_options.option_3: Replace system (recommended if unit is 15+ years old)
my_system_uses_r22_options.recommendation: If system is 15+ years old, replacement is usually smarter financial move

how_much_does_r22_cost.answer: Prices vary widely by region and supplier. Typically very expensive because production ended in 2020. Ask local HVAC contractor for current pricing.

retrofit_or_replace.answer: Simple rule: If system is 15+ years old, has a leak, or needs major repairs, replacement usually wins. If mechanically healthy and need short-term bridge, retrofit may be reasonable.
retrofit_or_replace.ask_for: Written estimate separating leak repair, retrofit labor, and expected performance changes

what_refrigerant_new_system.answer: Many new systems use R-410A (still available) or transitioning to R-454B or R-32 (A2L refrigerants). Ask your contractor about specific model.

first_questions: Is this a homeowner or technician?
What refrigerant does system use (R-22 or R-410A)?
Is there a leak, or just planning for future?
response_flow: 2. Clarify system age/condition and leak status
3. Present options: reclaimed R-22, retrofit, or replacement
4. Provide decision framework based on age, leak history, cost
5. Recommend next action (what to ask the tech, info to gather)
